transcript: [music] Hello. Hello everyone. This is Vaults for June [music] 17th, 2026. Can the UK stay [music] the course with its climate plans? I'm your host, David Roberts. By many measures, the UK is a genuine global leader on decarbonization. It passed an extremely ambitious climate change law in 2008 and has largely stuck with it since. It shut its last coal plant in 2024, making it the first G7 nation to leave coal behind entirely. It has cut its greenhouse gas emissions by almost 50% from 1990 levels, well ahead of some other countries one could mention. But its decarbonization push has recently hit some turbulence. The cross party consensus that held for 15 years seems to have come apart. With the Labor Party running the government, the Conservatives now want to repeal the very climate law they once championed and reform UK, which is leading in many national polls, is promising to scrap what they call net stupid zero. To make matters worse, America's ill-conceived Iran war has gas prices spiking and household bills heading back up. an awkward backdrop for a government elected partly on a pledge to cut household bills by 300 pounds by 2030. All of this agitta will be on display later this month when parliament votes on the UK's seventh carbon budget which will give the government guidance on how and how fast to continue cutting emissions. As proposed by the Labor government, it maintains course, but increasing concerns about industrial competitiveness and the cost of heat pumps and transport mandates will make this a spicier affair than past budgets. Can the government produce a credible policy package that meets the targets laid out in that budget? Now that the easiest reductions in the electricity sector are behind it, can it make similarly ambitious cuts in heating, transportation, and aviation, can it restitch its climate consensus and restore its global leadership? Much of that effort will fall to Katie White. For two decades, she has been a leading climate campaigner working at prominent NOS's to establish the UK's climate law and its basic policy architecture. Then in July 2024, she became a member of parliament and in September of last year, she was appointed the UK's climate minister. It's a rather dizzying ascent into the higher reaches of government. I can't wait to talk to her about how the climate effort looks from the inside and how she [music] intends to keep the country on track. [music] Katie White, welcome to Vaults. Thank you so much for coming. >> Thank you for having me, David. >> Let's start here. The most striking thing about UK climate politics at the moment from the outside perspective is that you know you were instrumental have been working for years with NOS's were instrumental in passing the climate change act way back in 2008 which passed on a crosspartisan basis and has had majority crossartisan support ever since up until it looks like right around 2023 when it looks like the consensus sort of fractured. Reform obviously is now out promising to repeal the whole thing. Conservatives are now following up saying they want to repeal the whole thing. It really looks like that consensus kind of flew apart pretty abruptly starting in 2023. And I'm just kind of curious, why do you think that happened? What is your diagnosis for what happened? >> Well, thanks David. That's a a great question. And in terms of where we are and why do I think it's happened? So, look, I don't think it's quite as broken as you might think or hear. Look, undoubtedly there has been some fracturing and you're right that in 2008 it was actually the Conservatives under David Cameron. David Cameron's leadership campaign was to call for a climate change bill and then the Labor government introduced it and they continue to deliver and in the last few years there's been some questioning about that and I very much have been taking a step back and and ask what is the questioning and what do we agree on and what do we disagree on and and what's the sort of problem with that and so I think look we are at a place in terms of there's a few bits to this one is our energy bills I think that's probably the main thing really and is certainly the number one issue for voters in the UK and it's the number one priority for our department. Secondly is energy security and thirdly is climate change and it's probably in that order that people really care at the moment. It's not to say they don't care about these things because I think the British public are the numbers who actually support action on climate change is incredibly high. But at the same time like most humans we want to have low bills and energy security and tackle climate change. So that's a we're trying to deal with that. And I think the case was made around climate change years ago. I'm not sure we've made the case particularly well. But also people, you know, energy bills have continued to rise and so people have made a sort of equation there of hold on a second, are these costs directly comparison? Now, you know, David, you're an expert on this. It's a very complicated picture. In the UK, what we're doing is we're moving from a a system where we've almost been renting energy. You know, it's been a commodity based system. We're buying off we have a certain amount ourselves, but broadly we're buying off other people, be it the US or Norway or other countries. We're buying oil and gas. And so we're now moving to an infrastructure model, although we're almost buying our own house. And there was a down payment cost on that. you know we have invested in terms of having wind turbines you know and actually the last government due credit to them they set a lot of this you know in in train the challenge is they also didn't do the grid infrastructure which meant that we haven't been able to take advantage of some of those costs so we were at that sort of tricky point in the transition and so I think we need to be honest about that there's also people have put politics all involved in it and you know obviously this is even more so in the states you know it's very much a binary has in left and right. I've never really agreed with that and and I'm very much happy to call things out on the left and the right and you know people want to have an argument about whether things have got a label or whatever. I don't really care about the labels frankly. I just want to make sure that we've got clean energy that is cheap, good for the UK and good for the planet. The other point and you might have some reflections in terms of where you think it is from where you're sitting but I was with a group of leaders yesterday and some who business leaders and some of them have been working on this for a while as well and they were saying actually a lot of this is cyclical and so there is generally cycles of support and and lack of support and we are for sure at a lower EB. They were reassuring me that it was going to go up. We'll see. Um but they did say that the troughs of those peaks the trough is getting higher as the trend is going up.
